moment_text: Experience that really, it's that point in life where you feel like, oh my God, I, I need more support. Postpartum was something that was incredibly challenging and it really redefined who I am. It was really hard, but I think it's like, you know, like a butterfly, right? Like you're going through this chrysalis and you, I think that some of the struggles have redefined me as a person, but that was really, uh, one of the most challenging parts of my life.

moment_text: You know, when you look at miscarriage and from a statistic point, it was like one in three women have miscarriage, so it's so prominent. And when I went through that, I, it was a shock, you know, culturally or, or how I, I was kind of, um. Told to manage that was, Hey, don't talk about your pregnancy until you're, you're in week 12. So not many people knew about it, which meant that I had not shared it at work, that I was pregnant. I had not shared it with like extended family or friends. Uh, but then I had this. Major loss and I was grieving and I was, I was incredibly sad. And I also felt like I was to blame, right? Because I was the one carrying the baby. And, uh, there were no tools to really navigate that. And I had to go, go through life like nothing had happened. When I was outside of my home, had to go back to work, had to interact with my friends, had to interact with family, but inside I was just really, really suffering.

moment_text: That was the first time in my life I felt like, oh my God, I can't, I can't do this. So that was incredibly hard, and luckily my doctors recognized that I was really struggling and they connected me to a social worker and a therapist. I got help immediately at that point, and so that helped me kind of cope with how I was feeling. And cope with the loss and the grief.

moment_text: So it is a very complex time in life. And what we do, ats, is we look at it holistically. So we, we can't just support your mental health and not think about how you're struggling with breastfeeding. We can't just support your breastfeeding, but not think about how you're dealing with it physically. So one of the things that we, we try to do is how can we provide mothers more support? And we've, uh, found a role called the postpartum doula role, which is reimbursed by Medicaid, and that allows us to meet moms where they are. In their home and support them hands-on support and build that trust with them so that we can now give them the education awareness, identify any concerns that they have, but also make sure that they are not alone in this journey.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2 with barrier signals (fragmented_care, social_isolation) and a clear system interaction (healthcare_provider), highlighting a systemic solution.
